speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Gustav Carlson, also known as Bud Carlson, took place at the home of his nephew near Troy on July 15th, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager. That spot, Bovard, down there in the canyon.

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh, Bovard. Bovard was never a town. Never even a store here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a mill?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, there was a mill later. But it was a, they had a telegraph station there, and oh, they had to have relays on these telegraphs. But the main thing is they put a sidetracking in there, had been put in there, for trains to meet on an old schedule, years before, so the trains could meet there. And then they fought, it was in 19, um,… 1911. Well,… i tell you, it began in 19,… uh, well, the winter of 19, 19, 9 and 10. And there was a fellow, um, who had to sell off land there, and they were, uh, he got rid of his places up on the top of the hill there. And there was some timber on them and some timber on the buried side. And, uh, end of Bovard is just below the end of Bovard, a little ways further down, a quarter of a mile, where the canyon flattens out. Well, they had a little telegraph station there, oh, ever since I think they installed the telegraph, the telegraph line. But that was about all. Well, it was, uh, I believe maybe those sidetracks were there so that they could drive cattle down and load them, if they had them, but as far back as I remember, they weren't used for anything. But they were gonna, they, uh, bought the rights to timber up the bare ridge side,… the log-in there off several places, and they, uh, milled there until it began. I think it is, uh, oh, about the winter of 1911 or so, they began moving in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They say they, who?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: They say Bovard Company, Bovard Lumber Company. It was locally owned by people around Troy that invested in the company.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it independent, or was it tied up with Troy Lumber?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: No, it wasn't Troy Lumber Company, because I don't believe the Bowmans were in it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But, uh, well, I think Nelson, now, what's his name?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Emil Nelson was the name of the man. I don't think he, well, he could be living now. But I believe he was, he was down there as director, I think he was, in Marona, the operations.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what was the access to, uh, to Bovard from Burnt Ridge?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: The access was pretty bad. There's an old road down the end of Burnt Ridge, about halfway down the canyon, that can be used. It went down to where that hay field lies there on the hillside, and around where there was the old house that was built by Colville. I mentioned that to you before. A Hagen family lived there, and I first remembered. And then there had been also a very old road, in which the rocks had just been rolled out of the way and dug in a little, down to the railroad tracks. So it evidently had been used once before to get access to that railroad. From there on, it was just, it just had to drive along the railroad. And sometimes in places, they had wagons going in there, they had to straddle one of the rails in order to find room. It wasn't, so they had to know when the trains were coming to get their stuff down. But that's the way they went on down. And around to the very edge of the point, and there they built a cookhouse. And they had a bunkhouse also built across on a stream there. They kicked the stream bed around and made a little dam across the that, so that they could just handle the logs and put in a very small saw there and handled it. And they turned, and for two or three years they were turning out the timber there. And then it was through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's all?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: I don't think so. I don't think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You say it was out by 1911?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, no, they started, they actually started in 1911. I think the name Sollwort didn't start until 1912. They had, I think there were a couple of cutters up there in the woods. I remember two of them that were cutting down the trees and putting them in lengths. Before that, early spring and winter. And they were getting ready to go there and that was, it was really about 1912 before they actually started.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did this kind of operation pay?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh, I think it paid. They sold the timber, they got the logs off, they used the donkey engine, you know what they were? Yes. You wind up with a cable and pull things down for a part of it and they used teams for part of it, pulling the logs in. They had that installed there and a little dam and a cookhouse and they built the bunkhouse up on what was like a little island between the two streams, one coming from Troy direction and one coming from the down Camp Canyon. Well, it was a crowded place to work on but they worked out and heed those sandbars around until they had a place for the lumber to be piled and then they ran a series of tracks and they feed it right into where they could run the cars in and load them right there. Oh, I don't know, they took out a lot of timber there. They worked there for a couple of years or so. Let's see, by 19, I think over, I think about 1914 they were through. A couple of years operation. They didn't have expensive machinery as far as that goes. It's not like these mills that are expected to go for years so they put in the simple stuff as they could. They knew about how long it would take. It probably took a little longer than they thought it would take.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Disemployed rich people or did they all come out to Troy?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: No, Burnt Ridges, I don't know if any Burnt Ridges, I don't think any Burnt Ridges worked down there. They were operating mostly in the summer when they were big on the farm. Well, they used to buy milk at our place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many guys would you guess would have been down there?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh,… five or six around the mill, the engine, and then the number of sawyers varied. Teamsters logging in, they didn't have the same amount all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would it all be timber from very close to the mill?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Very close, yes. They didn't go any farther up the ridges. They took some off the place that we bought, the Sanders place. And, you know, Kelberts I guess too. Kelberts place, that would be the next one. From the places on the Burnt Ridge side, that would have been partly Elif Johnsons and I don't think they went as far north as Redine, opposite the Redine place, but they went as far as the Porter. But I don't believe they ever got the Porter Canyon taken. They didn't, that timber wasn't sold, somebody didn't sell that timber to them. So it just about went up to what is now the edge of, the north edge of the Ernie Anderson's farm. And probably, maybe they went a little further on the Burnt Ridge side, I don't know about that. But I don't think so. We're only talking about a couple of miles. Oh yes, we're only talking about a couple of miles of timber and it wasn't all good timber on that area either. But there was quite a bit of heavy timber in there. It hadn't been timbered out before. It wasn't second growth. That had been passed over. The only thing that had been taken out was going down there and cutting a tree for some purpose, poles or something, and taking them away. So they mostly bought timber, not land. They didn't hold too much land as compared to the timber they bought. No, no, they didn't buy any land. They just bought the timber.

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: In fact, we bought 200 acres there in the winter of 19...9 and 10. The deal went through at the same time. They bought the timber for a certain price. We bought the land for a certain price. That's what it was for, land and timber separately.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it all canyon?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: It was all canyon. And it didn't quite include all our timber either. Because they bought it up to a certain line on a certain forage. And there was a little hump of good timber north on the west side of that line that does not include it. I think if you go down there today, you'll probably see that the timber is higher on that thing than below it. The second growth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: After you got the land, did you use it for pasture?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Yes, it was just part of pasture. That particular part hung in a steep canyon between the end of Calvary's place and the end of our place. And it wasn't much good for pasture. In fact, the whole hillside there was...cattle seldom grazed there. It was too much straight up and down. And this guy got brushed over with snow berry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the land much good to have then for you?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, that just came as part of lying on the same forage and yeast as some good land. At the top, why running through the top...let's see... The top 80 of that, or the top 120, had about 75 acres of good farming. At the top, that was 120. No, 40, and actually we split 180 diagonally, lying on one side of a creek. And then if you take 140 below that, that was only 80 acres. And actually there was about...most of that was plowable land. It was just that steep hillside, acreage going down there, that was not good land. The rest of it got all broken up eventually. About 70 acres there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you bought that land on top that had timber on it at the time, some of it?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Some of it was there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that was logged by this company?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Company, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Made it a little easier for you?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Yes, they took that off there, but there were big trees, left little trees and brush there. And we got some 11 or 10 acres also of canyon land and canyon orchards. It was planted in the hillside too, on more or less leveled out land, on benches in the canyons along with it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say canyon orchard, there was orchards there already or you planted?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: No, we didn't plant it. The fellow that homesteaded there, Colwood, he built that little house down on a flat. He had put an orchard up in some steeper ground above his house. Around the house there, he had garden and hay. But he planted the orchard up in the hillside there. I think it's probably still there. I don't know if they've taken care of it or not over these years. We used to pick things down there. The fact is we once, oh a few times, we had such a heavy pair crop down there that we hauled boxes of pairs to town for several days there. Two loads a day for town after they were picked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know these fellows, these families that lived in the canyon there, did they turn over more? Was that a rougher place for people to make a go of?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: It wasn't a place where anybody could make a living. They all worked for something else. But they had a passion to own a piece of ground and live there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what I'm wondering is, did they stay? Did the people own their stay?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: No, none of them stayed any length of time. They were isolated, hard to get to town. Tough road out, they're tough road down. And they just quit and all moved away. The fact is, in my memory, and I think I can remember it, I hadn't started school yet, I know that. That fellow Hagen, a railroad worker, he rented the place. And just to keep his family, and he worked for the railroad. He didn't buy in, but I guess he found it was too inconvenient a place to keep his family and get a market for them. He could raise some garden, I guess maybe that's what induced him to...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hagen worked for the railroad, would he just hike down the boulevard and pick up a train?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: I expect, well I think he, they were on the flat, they had these little flat cars, you know. He was probably working in that area part of the time between Troy and Kendrick. So he probably could get on at Bovard more or less. Yes, or any place where they ditched their flat cars, the old man cars, you know, that they used to use. They could pump up and down and make them run.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think these guys were, these families were thought of as, by the Burnt Ridgers as being kind of outside the community?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, they were there early, some of those fellows, before some of the Burnt Ridgers came. The present, the ones that I remember, they were recent comers as compared to these people that built houses in the county and the homestead. I don't know, most people were already surrendered and left, finally to the poor place. And they homesteaded some of them. Mm-hmm, they wasn't homesteaded. Oh, they had an idea that all land as it began to develop was going to be valuable someday. They didn't know how long it was going to be, but they were optimists, I guess. I know everybody always speculated, what in the world they went down to those canyons for and built houses. That was the general consensus opinion for them. Most people couldn't understand what they came over them to do that. They learned too, they weren't in a very good place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think that the reason might have been that they were not farmers?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, they probably weren't farmers. No, as far as I know, they didn't work at farming. I don't know what Chusick and Monahan worked at regularly. I think they did mostly teamster work. They would take them away from their places and leave their families there. In Hagen, he worked at a covalent. I don't know, I don't think he stayed up there. I think he gave them up on his place and was ready to sell it. Sanders bought it before he left. Sanders left about 1906. He bought that canyon, man. That's why our buy amounted to 200 acres that time. He only had originally 160. We got 120 of that and another 80 acres. I mean that amount there. Well, he got it very cheap, you know. I doubt they wanted to get rid of it for any dollars they could. It made better pasture for his cattle. He took a few cows. Actually, there are no fences down there. Separating the two of these cows had pastured over that. Anyway, I think it was probably in order to keep somebody else from buying up that area. And, uh, fencing it off and cutting him off from pasture. Which he was actually using because nobody put out any fences.

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: It would pay him to have done that. I wonder if they expected the road would go through to, you know, Potlatch. To the Potlatch from there. They expected that that area would go through. Yes, they wondered. The only feasible route, besides the one over American Ridge down to Kendrick, would be to run one right down the railroad tracks from Troy to Kendrick. Or else, if they wanted to come over to Burnet Ridge, it would be to take off down that draw, which is right at the end of what was our place, which is Trout's now. His and Anderson's. And follow that thing right down, that is the level of the grave there. And come down to the creek bottom there. It would have road that way they'd have about, uh, You know, they would have saved the distance between the bovard, where the bovard mills did, and Troy, of New Road, which would be used to burn to Ridge Road. And it isn't more than about three miles down from there. There is an old side tracking called Clyde Spur. And there they had warehouses built up, or it was a grain receiving town. And there was a road down from the Bear Ridge, and I think one from the American Ridge. Because there were some bench farms in there on the American Ridge that were closer that way than anywhere else. And they used to come down over the Bear Ridge Grades and unload their wheat there. And they had good warehouses. Oh, I saw them, oh, the last time, I suppose, About nineteen thirty or so, and they were still standing, you know, Standing just fine there. Those had been well built, looked like no deterioration at all in those, uh, Those great warehouses. Storehouses, I suppose, didn't cause. And from Clyde Spur, then how far was it down to, uh, It reviled over about three miles forward to Kendrick, and there was a road that ran down that way. From Clyde Spur to Kendrick. There was a road that had been laid out, you know, that had never, I suppose, deteriorated from lack of use. Because it was that, uh, taking on grain from down there died out. They had a better road down over the end of Bear Ridge, right into Kendrick, and they'd do that. And the American Ridge had a grade into Kendrick. All that. Some of those things, other kinds of roads, you know, They was all just dirt and horse manipulated, were pretty bad sometimes. And that probably accounted for Clyde Spur. A little bit shorter haul.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And, uh, probably one of the farmers would man the warehouse or something?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh, they'd get somebody there to man the warehouse. Uh, actually, there happened to be a little house. And, uh, long after they had abandoned taking the wheat down there, they had occupants. It was just, it went up a little, oh, hundred yards on a little slope. It flattened out quite a little space before it went up again. And there'd been an orchard built there, and there'd been some land broken out. Probably mostly for a hay field or berries planted, and things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This is right above Clyde Spur?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Just above Clyde Spur. I know that it was very late there were people living there. And, uh, I suppose, uh, I suppose it either went up by, really, a bare ridge to, uh, or else went down to Kendrick from there. Somebody occupied that house for some time. Oh, I think even in the grain hauling days, maybe it was at that house, or maybe somewhere else, somebody used to have a little coffee and sandwich stand down there. The grain holders wanted a cup of coffee before they went back up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: By Clyde Spur there was one, huh?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: That's something. But Boulevard was really only a siding before the mill. It was a siding, as far as I know it ever was, it was a telegraph station. And they used to have a telegraph operator down there. I don't understand all that much about the telegraphs in that time, but they must have been after relay things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was stationed there and lived there?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: No, he wasn't lived there. He walked down from Troy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There was a little office there for him or something?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: No, just no real office at all. It was just a place for the city in there. Spent some hours there. I don't know what, but I guess maybe he had to give reports on how the trains were coming through on the time. This was when you were a boy. When I was a boy. I think, uh, I don't know if it was there after night. I know by the time they started that mill he was gone. They didn't use it anymore. I don't know if the little building was still there or not. It was more or less built like these phone booths that you see on the streets.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear any stories about the people that lived in the canyon?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: No, no particular story. I, uh, no I didn't. I think there's a Tuesday stayed around there a while. I believe people may have known him. Bruce's dad, he's about 12 years older than I was and well, I think he can tell you whether that or not. But I believe that they were there after we came to the farm before they were able to remember. Because, uh, I know he got acquainted with the Tuesdays. And his son-in-law, Monahan, built another house up a little further. He built a better house than any of the rest of them. And he did the same thing. Built a little orchard around his house on a flattened bench. And there was never any raid garden there. The soil had gone back to brush springing up and all that. And I saw it. You'd see there probably had been bled out at one time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, the thing that this, uh, talking about these canyon people makes me think about is, uh, The geographical set up in the country out there with the ridges and then the steep canyons, that had a lot of effect on the way people lived, didn't it? I mean, that was quite a barrier, those canyons, wasn't it?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Yes, they were. Actually, it cut them into communities. People on one side of the canyon formed a community like American Ridge. And, uh, on the other side like Bird Ridge, the other side like Little Bear Ridge. Those people all knew each other much better than they, uh, did the people on the other ridges. And the schoolhouses, of course, had to be made with some thought as to how many, how the kids could get there. So they said, well, they had a couple of schoolhouses on Little Bear, Longer Ridge, and Big Bear. I think they had even more in American Ridge. Bird Ridge being the shorter one had only one. And the people, the kids at the upper end of that place probably often went to Troy to go out of close. That didn't bother them either. So, um...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like, um, getting across the canyons from one ridge to another?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, I tell you, we used to go across there, in horseback riding.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But we'd regularly cease with that, too, isn't it?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: But, uh, there were trails that had already been sort of used. One led from our place down west to the canyon where they had a gate in the, uh, gates in the railroad, um, railroad fence. And a gate on the other side. And then we crossed the creek down there. There was a mystery rough crossing. And there was one that led on a slant all the way around. A fairly easy slant for about that, to come up that draw where the old Harlan place was. Oh, once in a while I think probably some of those people met each other when they used to be on the fish. Because there used to be much more water in those streams and they could catch fish through the summer. The Compton family, there was an old family on there, they were just about opposite us. And just below them, the Harlan family. And the Cummings family straight across the other way south.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: I'm talking about my other one, but I wanted to get mine.

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: They usually meet in Troy and sometimes got acquainted. My brothers got acquainted first over there. The reason is that on the other side of American Ridge is Driscoll Ridge, the upper part, but it ceases. And after that, further down on American Ridge is Fix Ridge. And I had two uncles that had farms over there, my father's brothers. And that was the shortcut to get to over there. No road, we had to go a long ways around by road up through Troy. And down as far as, well, as Millers-Trussell down here. And then take the road south to get there. By wagon road. So we used that trail down there and came up on American Ridge. And went down the other side by Bethel Church. And followed a trail down that canyon on the side that after you came opposite, the farms that my two uncles had. They had farms that were just about extended from Fix Ridge to the bottom. And there were trails up there too that had been used for crossings. There seemed to be quite a few trails for crossings. I don't remember a good trail for crossing from Brook Ridge to Bear Ridge, but they made it. By going down the end of the ridge and finding a way up that hillside there. Because I know they rode over that way to Hups a good deal. Hups was the last family on the little Bear Ridge. Like we were the last family, I don't know, it was the last land on Bird Ridge. Usable land.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long would it take you on horseback if you wanted to cross one or the other?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh, I don't know. Oh, they took several hours. How long did there go standing down on the same... Yes, you'd go down. You had no place where you could run a horse. It's too steep down one, too steep up the other. The trails weren't that wide either or that smooth. You'd probably take the hill and walk all the way. You see down the hillside, there'd be hours down. You'd have to stay with the dash and the other thing. It would be closer to half a mile than a quarter. And the same up the other side.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And across the bottom?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, across the bottom that was very little. The railroad track there occupies one side and the creek next to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you're talking about traveling a little over a mile?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: A little over a mile. About a mile to get up the other... Oh no, it didn't take that long. You'd get across the top to American Ridge in an hour and a half. But then... They had a longer canyon to cross to get over to my... Oh, they could start sometime during the day and get there before night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When your mother went mid-wifing, would she walk or ride?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, we generally tried to see that she'd get a ride, but sometimes things came up to... I don't know the early ones that they had there. There was sometimes a lack of horses for you. It had a few horses on the farms. But there was another family that came at a later time and built a canyon house. Moody. Pete Moody. And he hosted it at 160 acres there and it had just very little farmland on top. About an acre or two. But he hosted it for the timber because he knew that a mill was going in. It was on the American Ridge side, the timber was. And he sold his timber to the mill. He and a fellow next to him had canyon land with timber on it. And that was a mill too. That mill was located up between Beauvoir and Troy. It would be a good mile and a half of the distance of Beauvoir in the direction of Troy. That was called Hall's Mill, Hall-Rannins. He was just an individual contractor. He set up a small mill and it worked there for a couple of years. And Pete Moody was there and he worked right for the mill. He was a woodsman. He cut the trees and had them all ready for them to drag down off his farm. And so he got quite a bit for his timber. And he worked for them and brought them off to the other places.

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: One of his daughters, I think, lives in town yet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was one of his children that died in the early years that he was there?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh, there were two children that died down there. And one who was grown up died from, oh, I don't know, violent bleeding. Caused by, I don't know, just what. He had about seven children. I think about four of them were living. One of them ran that little, you know where that little, what's it called, hose apples now? They made it all entirely into a restaurant now. But it used to be kind of a variety store with a coffee counter. And she and her husband, or she ran it actually. I never saw her husband there. She ran that for a couple of times. Just lately I think she's probably still around town. Hmm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, talking about dying children, what about tuberculosis? Was there a problem?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Some of them had tuberculosis, yes. Mr. Auler, whose place was just north of our, had drabs to hold it down. His wife died of TB. She had TB. Her brother, I think, had it too, but he, that was Kelper, was her brother. But he managed to survive until he was near 80, I guess. Anyway, he sort of overcame it. But in that family now, that second daughter, Mindy, two older, elder daughters that come to TB. You know, Mindy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which family is this now, the Kelper family?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Ernie Anderson's wife was the third daughter in that family. And Helen and Mindy, there are pictures of all of them when they were in school. Also comes TB. And the Pearson family, two of those boys got TB. Willie Pearson and Walter Pearson, the two oldest boys. They lived where Rudolph Smith lives now. That was 160 there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were those sicknesses around the same time, or spread over?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, they seemed to have contracted them somewhere in the same time. But Mrs. Auler, I think, had been infected with it when she came out. She was sickly then. Though she seemed to have maintained pretty good health for a number of years. I think she had when she arrived from Oklahoma. That's where they came from.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people think that's how it got started on the Ridge?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, no, I don't know whether it got started. There were other people in other places around town, other ridges that had TB, too. It just seemed to be something that people got in those days and didn't know how to take care of it. The kind of treatment they had didn't seem to help them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they do?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, one of them did very little. So few got out and they sent Hilis. When they found Hilma had TB, they sent her to a sanitarium in Colorado, there she was for some time. I don't know. Somehow or other it didn't... She didn't improve. They kind of kept her at the level for a while. She died at about 30. She got it active again. She'd had it somewhat, maybe in her second. But she got it active. She was teaching school out there at the place and her brother was going to take and farm an area over on Texas Ridge. Where the... That Kelberg had bought. He bought some land over there. Except that I don't know, maybe the family still owns it. But they had bad weather and she was working hard helping him with everything. And it was inconvenient. Oh, household. Household there, they had to take and carry waters from the well there. They had never been installed in that place yet. Rather old primitive structure on that land. And she got awful colds. And probably didn't stop working to take care of them at first and got worse and worse. And she was just sick. And her active case of TB came afterwards.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Came back?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: It all came on afterwards.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, had she had it before?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Not that they ever do. But it just seemed to... maybe a later system, dormant. I do contest an ordinary healthy person and they find that there's TB in them. But it doesn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did she live after that?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh, I think she lived only about... I think she lived three or four years. They had her in Sanitarium in Colorado. But she'd evidently taken it awfully bad before she ever took treatment.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you had TB and you were going to be treated at home, what could they do for you? Did they have you sleep in a separate place?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Yes, they liked to isolate everybody. There was a distorted family rented that land for about two years. When I was about nine and ten years old that we later bought. They rented it and ran only at them. And they had a daughter that died of TB there. She was married and had four children. But her husband, they didn't really have a place where he could keep her. And they took her out and put her in a tent there. And she lived... Oh, she only lived part of a year after that. She was pretty bad. Well, I don't know what his work was, but I believe he was a railroader too. I didn't know at that time that he was dying of cancer. At the same time as his wife was dying of TB. But he kept on working as long as he was able to. He had four small children. I guess he thought he had two. So, I wonder what happened to the children. Well, the children lived on with their grandparents until about the time they left Burnt Ridge. And then, it was too much for the old folks to turn to it. They were not young at all, their grandparents were. And the sisters and brothers of their daughter who died of TB, divided the children up among them. And adopted them into their families, raised them at least. I don't know where they were divided, exactly.

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Except I know that Grace, the second girl, was raised by Frank Lyon of Texas Reach. He was married to one of the sisters.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Whose sister was she now?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: She was the sister of the girl that died. Her sister, the woman that died of TB, she had four sisters, I think, and three brothers. And a number of these picked up the four children. I don't know whether any of them took two or not. He kept the children while he was alive and working. Well, actually, he left with the grandparents there a while and came back and visited and helped support them by paying. And he died less than a year. One of the other sturderers told me that years later, I didn't know it at the time. We sort of wondered about him, why he didn't find a place for his kids. But that was the explanation, all right. He was a dying man and had to make it the best of what he could. Worked as long as possible and then died in some hospital… people like the Sturdivants,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that they were really, I mean, for her to be living in a tent when she was sick?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: In the summer. Oh, the summer. Yes, the summer or late spring, I guess, when she brought her there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that they were just perhaps too poor to be able to try to do anything else?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, they didn't have money. They hadn't had money at all. And the doctor did what he could for her and prescribed treatment. But I don't know, they didn't do so much either those days. I wonder what kind of treatment they had. I don't know that. I haven't had any idea really what they did. Except they wanted them in fresh air and away from them. They wanted clean air for them and away from other people for infection purposes and people to keep things clean. And being probably the same kind of sanitary precautions that they take now. Well, they weren't equipped like you did a hospital to do that, so it probably fell short.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think there was much concern about it spreading?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh, yes, there were very much concern. There was a good deal of concern. I don't know. My dad was worried about it. We played with those Calvary children. And he, well, he said, stay outdoors. Don't go in the house as much as you can. Stay out in the fresh air if you play with those kids. The Pearson boys, too, I don't know. It was all getting cold and I think each one of them had a case of pneumonia before they got TB.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was sending to a sanitary considered the best thing you could do?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, yes, they did. Now, I don't know whether Will had ever been to a sanitary or not, but maybe his case was like coma. He found that he wasn't getting any better and so he left and he lived in a separate room in his own home there. He was a fellow who took precautions himself to see that he kept away from infecting people. Walt, he got it later. He was his younger brother by about two and a half years or so, I guess. He went down to California. I think he went to one of the sanitarians of Arizona first. I'm not sure that that was true, but he came back to Pasadena and lived there. Actually, he had done well in farming before he got this. He died at about 33 and he bought himself a good place there. The sister that was next to him in age was unmarried, one of his sisters. She came down there and stayed with him. She got the house. He gave her the house. Paid it up, paid in full for her when he died. She had that house for years afterwards. She lived to be a very old lady, about 86 or 87, so she wasn't...

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: I wonder how tuberculosis did spread, how people thought that it did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you have to catch it from somebody else?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: It was a general feeling, I don't know. I don't know all there is to know about TV. You compare it with today when it's not hardly a problem at all. No, it has been cut down tremendously now. I heard that there was a man who was killed. I think just too many people were carriers of some kind. They were strong enough to throw it off and not get sick. But it was there and he would come out and maybe he would attack somebody else without knowing it. I heard that a man was killed in a logging accident on the ridge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember that?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh yes. That I think was the voice. One of the boys died before he reached maturity. The other one lived to about 86 years old or something like that. Right down here is Troy. It wasn't too many years ago that if I was up here I talked to him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you hear a story about horse falling in a well? A horse falling in a well?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Yeah, on the ridge. Well we had a horse fall into an old cistern once and we managed to get it out. I didn't know there was any story on the protection. I heard that there was one but it wasn't your horse. It was another. I think it was another one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened that time though? How did your horse fall in the cistern?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh it was a boarded across well but he was curious and got up there and pondered away with his house probably on the boards and they broke. They weren't bent at all. It was a little platform you know. A boarded across. Curious sort of horse. Okay, my back. He's a poor, mean, married kid. He didn't know Paul. Right. You know Marvin?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't really know him.

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well his grandfather, on his mother's side, Fredland, he died in a well digging accident. I don't know how. I don't think it was drilling. I think they were digging, hand digging a well. I don't know any details of that but that was before. I'm not even sure it happened after we came to the ridge in 99 or it was before I knew anything about it. I don't know anything old enough to know anything about the people around me. Because I always remembered there was a widow. His wife.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how he died in the accident? What had happened?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: No, I don't know just what happened.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much water witching down on the ridge for wells?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: I never heard of witching down there. Maybe somebody did. But I heard of witching in different places either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Around this country you mean?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh yes. Yeah well I thought people did in most places. I'm surprised they didn't do it on Burton. I never heard of it because they had any water witches down there. Most people never put any stock in it. As far as I know I never heard of somebody witching down there. Maybe they did find some of those wells by witching. I couldn't say. I'm sure nobody on that ridge claimed to be a water witch. They had to have some outsider to come in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where do you think it was concentrated? You know, believing in water witching?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: I don't know. I heard of my sister-in-law who just died. Jill's grandmother. She knew of it being done over on Merkovich among those people over there. I guess there are quite a few people that would witch around here for water now. I know there was a water witching done from Mike Redine's big well out there. He found it where the witches said it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have a good water supply in your place?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: We had one good water supply. One very good spring. It never ran dry. Very early on when I was about five years old, we installed a little dam under that. It was sufficient to have water in it to operate in a hydraulic ram. We laid pipe up the hillside. It was down on the canyon side. Up the hillside and put it in a reservoir. Up there. That way we got water in the house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't that a little unusual to have a hydraulic ram?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, it wasn't the first ones. Dad had seen them operate somewhere else before we put that one in. It appeared to be working alright on the big spring down on one of the other ridges. That was the only one I think that was ever installed. Most of those springs on the sides were not big springs. I never saw any spring on that ridge along the hillsides that compares to that spring in size. There were other springs that ran smaller streams that they used. Building a little protection around them and making a trough, for cattle they were usable. There were several little springs on our fence that worked from time one. One was fairly steady. It probably still is. Just a small stream, just like a trickle of coffee out of that little percolator out there that we have. But it was steady. If I'd have wished for water, I'd have wished up in that hillside above it. I think that was where it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But did you have enough water for your needs?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: We had enough water for our needs at the house and yard for all the cattle and everything like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Off that hydraulic ram?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, yes. We could store it up in the tank and keep it running enough to keep the tank about full. It would gain on us. If we had it running steady, it always gained. Kept sending the water higher and higher to an overflow pipe we had. Sometimes we just let the overflow pipe go. I know when Bruce's parents lived on that farm for a time, they hooked a hose to the end of it and used it to irrigate a little garden there to keep it going good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you hear about the ice caves?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: They're just about opposite Clyde Spur, which I referred to a lot. Ice mine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where opposite? Which direction?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: On the American Ridge side. Just about opposite Clyde Spur. I was never in them. Sanders knew about them when we came there. My father accompanied him down here once and I think my brother Carl went with him. They looked in those ice caves. I believe unprotected, the rains would wash down mud and cover the entrance to them too. So I don't know if they'd be exposed to them. I expect they're probably still there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they impressed by them?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Yes, they thought they were something. I heard of them first. I never did get down there to see them. I don't know why they had somebody to guide me to the spot if they were covered over. But there were a lot of people that did not know about them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever have that French for a teacher?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Yes, here's his picture. He taught about four years I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he the fellow that you were mentioning to me that talked to students about current affairs?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Yes, he was good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he the one?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Yes, he was the one. I think he really talked more than most teachers did. He was stuck entirely with the books. With regard to classes, he grouped them a lot too. If he'd have those that sometimes in two or three grades strung out on reading and certain things, he could find it read in one class and had to do arithmetic in another. It was all like that. He managed to consolidate grades one to eight, like they had to have there. So he didn't have to have a class for every grade, a class in every subject for every grade. It seemed to work just about as well. In fact, I think some of them had faster that way. They weren't kept to single grade when they could actually perform a little bit better. They were performing the grade above and learned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that he cared about the students more than some teachers?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: I think he did. He was interested in teaching all right. He liked teaching. Do you know, I heard a story about him that he used to walk out to school reading a book. Yes, yes he did. I saw him doing it many times. He had found a way to walk from Troy and cut off things. He'd come down the railroad track just about opposite the schoolhouse where he took a trail up and came following the fence down there. Then I would see him sometimes. He would be reading a book as he walked along. One thing about him, he was a fast walker. He was small in length, short, slender. But he made that trip in a hurry from when he started out walking. He just ate up the ground at a steady speed, fast speed. He was quite active. He started playing ball to the kids on recess. He was quick and active. You know, a couple of other people from the area told me that he was an excellent teacher. That he was really outstanding. He stood out in their minds too. Yes, he stood out. I think in his minds, Jill's grandmother went to school with him. She mentioned that to me. Yes, she thought he was pretty good too. We liked him on Burnt Ridge. We recognized he was much better than anything he'd had around there up to his time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Whatever happened to him?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, he was very much interested in this Nazarene church down here. He and his wife went down to South Idaho and they were starting in northwest Nazarene. It started from a sort of a school and built up into a college. It was a high school and an elementary school. They may still be there. It started in his first grade through. Yes, right on through. I don't think the guy did any four-year real college work for a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He taught down there?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: He taught down there and that for quite a few years. Eventually, he went to California. One of the boys told me to let him down there and he knew more about him. Well, actually it was because of a relationship. His sister married a brother of the Ruberg family. Yes, an uncle of the Ruberg children are here. There are several Ruberg children here. One, two, three. Their uncle married his sister. That was during the time that he was teaching out there. Both of them are living elsewhere. The family didn't know anything about it until just after it happened. For that reason, they were more or less in touch with their uncle and he was in touch with his sister. They said he went into real estate down there for a while. He became a real estate man. I know Willa thought he was an awful lot better than his brother. Bert was the famous... Bert was the... Congressman. Congressman. His brother Frank taught school. Maybe he meant Frank. Frank was younger than Carlton. Burton was Carlton's brother. Yes, he was. He was a little bit older, not much, than he was. Burton made Congress a rather youthful age. He had a long career there. A still older brother became a doctor. He finished as dean of the medical school in the University of North Dakota.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that at this period of time that women teachers hadn't hit the rural schools as much as they did later?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: No, there were still quite a few men. I think probably the majority were still women. At that time. Even at that time only there were more men. Men after it just disappeared. Not many years after he was one of the last ones, I think. This was 1910 that he was teaching. Well, he continued on. I think he left there about 1913. And I think he was one of the last of the men teachers in the schools around the Latah County here. The country schools around Latah County that he was. I think the others have dropped out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you think the men stopped teaching in the rural schools?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh, that his wages were low. He could make more at most anything. He could make more logging than he could teaching. Or working in the mill. And the others, I think, the big group. They would have a tough time on supporting the family on that. Fritz worked always during the summers, I know that. He worked sometimes in the old saw mill in Troy. They used to have a planing mill there and a saw mill both working together there. He worked in the planing mill down there. He worked on carpentry with somebody that was building houses. He was not the somebody on the, he worked for somebody and building houses. So he was constantly at work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How strict was he as a teacher?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, not really strict. But he did, the kids didn't get away with, he didn't care too much if somebody spoke to somebody else during that. Only if they made a habit of just playing, then he would suddenly... The mind was vague. He never had any difficulties. The kids liked him enough to be fell in with him. And they were not an obstreperous group out there at all. Pretty mild compared to some of the kids, even in their own time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you think that was? Think that's because of the parents?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: The parents, yes. They found the kids not getting along in school. They'd be more afraid of their parents than they were the teachers. What they'd have to, might do about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of obedience was expected by the parents?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh, expected pretty total obedience, most of them, I think. Probably didn't always get it. I'm curious about that, because that's the sort of thing that you can't put a finger on anymore. Nowadays, the way kids are raised is real different than then. It's permissive. Well, I don't know. How much would a parent... Well, they generally didn't interfere with the fun and play and all that sort of thing. Games and all that. Well, they wanted the kids to behave themselves. They wouldn't stand for a kid being insulting to people and things like that, talk up. They found a kid being sassy to some neighbor of theirs, and for no good reason. Why, he'd probably get a darn good lick of it, probably.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about to the parent?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, you...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you call your father, sir?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: No. Well, no. We just talked to him. You know what I mean, you know. Well, we... Oh, he told us to do that, that's about all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you need to be told very much what you needed to do?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh, quite a bit, quite a bit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You expect you waited for the order to come?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh, well, with regard to work and things like that, we knew what had to be done, yes. You only start like that. Well, it was sort of... I don't... It was pretty hard to know exactly what went on in your neighbors' private homes. But it was a rule, they were pretty respectable acumen when they were out among others. Oh, they'd... some were a little harder than others. That's where I suppose... They were among the young men, a few fights that took place in the ninth time. About all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your mother, for instance, in your family, was your mother easy on the kids?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh, yes, she was easy. She was greatly concerned with the welfare of the kids, of the first thoughts. I've told you about those mills on Beauvoir, the ones further up toward Troy. But there must have been an awful lot of small mills here and there around the country, I don't know where they all are. I know that years later I talked to one of the old timers on American Ridge, and he told us where a mill was been working there. And he'd do one of the bird ridge old settlers over there, because he was there as a young man, and he'd worked that mill before he'd... He'd home-screw a lot of bird ridge, out of the Clarence Johnson spot. So there was milling over there, too, they'd shuttled the old fields here and there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about that Oakay-Allison mill?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: That ran for quite a while. Oh, that ran for a long time. I don't know when it even stopped, I don't know. I don't know when it ceased there again. I heard that some of the Burnt Ridgers worked there. Oh, some of them undoubtedly did, yes. Yes, that's close by. It went down there in wintertime. Quite a few of them sometimes got employment, something like that. You know, I wanted to ask you a little more about laying out, and, you know, of school and the effects of that. And I was thinking about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that, if you looked at the kids on the ridges that you knew about, do you think that most of the boys, or just some of the boys, would lay out for a period of time?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: A large part of them did. When they reached a certain age. You said twelve. Twelve, thirteen, fourteen. Twelve, maybe, as early as twelve, sometimes, some stayed, they said later.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you're talking about laying out, what kind of a time period would the kid be laying out for?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, in the fall, to get help, helping when the fall crops were being planted, the fall work done. In the spring, when the spring planting began, the spring planting. Those were the periods that they were out, sometimes at both ends.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And how long might that be in the fall?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh, they might miss a month and a half of school in the fall, and a month and a half in the spring. Or somewhere around that, some of them stayed out more.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would the teacher have any say-so about something like that?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: I think they were, but of course, I think they mostly ignored it. Because they knew how the situation was, they didn't. And it would be a kind of a fuss, probably, if they did. It wasn't until about 1912 that they laid down stricter state law, because undoubtedly for the same reason, that they found too much of it. Too many people were out. And then they, I think they just sought to cease with the new strictures that they put on it. They laid penalties on it, but it wasn't happening.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This wouldn't affect the girls, though, just the boys?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, there were girls, too, that were much affected.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There were?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: There was, for instance, our neighbor, a North of us, Oliver.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: His wife died when, oh, what was this?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: I imagine the oldest girl was only about 14. She hadn't, and for quite a long time before that, her mother was six a bunch of the time, and she stayed home. A great deal of the time. And at that time, I don't know, she didn't go to school for a while until the farmer got better situated. And he sent all these girls away to school. And I think Minnie eventually, I don't know whether she finished university, but she drew teaching certificates, the same girl who had the layout, and taught until she was married. And the younger, one of the younger girls, well, one of the younger girls went on to school quite a while and married young. And another one became a teacher also and was on until she retired, reached retirement age. She was a hell of a very good job there, just state supervisor of home economics training in Wyoming.

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: But now, there's the other family, this girl I said, this guy, the Tuberculosis Man's guy, it's normal, but she was out of school a great deal. Because her mother was having one baby after another those years, and she was the oldest girl, and one brother was older, and ten children younger. And you can imagine that she stayed home to help her mother and stayed out when we had the children born. And if they were overloaded with the work around there, she stayed home a great deal. She didn't finish grade school until she was seventeen, as a result. And in light of her intelligence, there was no excuse for it, except not having had enough schooling. But she went on, she went through high school in three years then, and took a couple of years to the normal school, the teachers college then, two years, and was teaching for a number of years. And that was what happened to her that one summer, she was helping her brother out on this farm, and she got terribly laid up from just overwork and exposure, and never stopping, and she had bad goals, and got the TVs going. Well, it looks like there was a, maybe you could say it was a kind of a conflict between desire for education and the demands of the work. A lot of it was. In the case of some of the little people it was, some of them did not care for school and had no ambition to go on to high school and things like that, but they didn't know anything about it. They both tended to be new at the time, at the present time. Well, I think they would all have been coming to school for high school, and it doesn't go well with anybody else, and quite a few of them would have continued.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that there were cases, many cases, where a family would send the kid to school and had hardship to what the kid, the youngster, could be doing if he was at home, healthy?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh yes, well there's a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What I'm wondering is, would a kid go to school, if there was a case of hardship, would school always lose out, you know, or would the work lose out sometimes too?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, a little of both, but I believe the hardship won out quite a bit. Also, the attitude toward education. We didn't really believe that education was for a person of ordinary talents. We had a great respect for education, and we took the awfully smart person to get it to stand in education. That was just a belief, it bugs some of the older people. They didn't really conceive of farmers' kids as being themselves, and farmers' kids as themselves were secluded as being the intellectual variety. They were plenty smart. They were done all right in any competition, most any competition in college today. Well you know, you take like your family, and you were telling me that your two oldest brothers bore the brunt of clearing the land. Now, then when it comes to the third brother, Joe, he got to go away to school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what happened? Is it because the land was already cleared up?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, it was sort of cleared up and things were going better, we were getting crops and getting more money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it was a matter of the economic situation?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Economics, yes, economics, yes. And Homer Peterson, the teacher down there, went down to our farm one day and told my dad, if he could stand it, he ought to get Joe an education. He tried to plant it, hit him. I think that influenced Dad too. He was glad to hear that he had a boy that teacher thought that was Joe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think that's what did it?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, it did influence him, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when Joe went to Lewiston, was it the prep part of Lewiston? Did he work when he was down there?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, he didn't. Part of the time, yes. He had jobs and different things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he keep going after he finished the prep part?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Yes, he kept on going and he taught in, oh, before he got his degree, he taught about a couple of years at the country school. And then he went on to, well, he taught a year at Winchester in the grade school there and went to Bonners Ferry. He became principal of the grade school there and went to Douglas from there, Douglas, Arizona, principal of the grade school. And it wasn't until after the war that he went back and he went to several summer schools in California, once at Columbia University in New York. But it wasn't until after he came back from World War I that he took a year at the University of Washington and got a degree out of it. He got a double degree that time, a Bachelor of Arts and a Bachelor of Education.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much older was he than you?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: About nine years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now what about your two older brothers? How much education did they get?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, they didn't take any formal education. They had to have a thing because they were too old for education. So, Walter, one couple of winters, went up and attended a business college in Spokane. And he used it for a little while, but not right away. But he went up to, he did farming, he was the best he had, he had a farm down here most of his life. Now Walter went up to Calgary and after working around there for a while, he looked at some land there and actually filed a claim, but he decided it was too cold a country to live in. He came down to Montana, which is too much warmer, and there he settled. Oh, he was about, oh about 30 by that time. And then he bought a relinquishment from a fellow who wanted to get out and then he just held onto that land and he bought more land. Well, he kept on farming it to himself for quite a few years and then he was getting pretty, well, a little hard and tough to handle the machinery. That bothered him, the constant rattle of machinery and tractors and that. He would come out with a headache every night. So he, the last 10 years I guess of his life, he, well more than that, I think he was about 15 years, 16 years. When he was around 60, he rented it out. Well, he used to stay up there around there in the summers in Montana, like where all the friends he'd made over that had lived. And he'd go down mostly to Long Beach, you know, down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did either he or Paul, did they get through the 8th grade or what?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, they were probably through the 8th grade as far as working is concerned. But that is a funny thing too. Nobody seemed to know when they were through the 8th grade or how they got out of the 8th grade. They didn't, I think it was, some of those teachers never informed the kids that there was such a thing as state examinations, as there were in those days. At least nobody ever, they never brought out, they never trained a class to do that. French was the first one to do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To prepare kids for exams?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Yes… oh, three kids when Steelsmith was there learned about when the examinations were coming, they went somewhere, they went across to America Ridge. They rode horseback across the canyon on those trails I told you about and took the examinations over there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Paul one of them?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: No, that was Carl. Carl, that's right, because Mrs. Carlson, Willa Carlson told me that Carl had gone over to America. Yes. There were two other boys there. And one thing about it, there were some classes he had never given any classes in, what corresponded to the 8th grade, and that was in government. When he learned that they were going to want to take that examination, he advised them to read the Constitution of the United States, which was in the back of the history books. Sounds like good preparation. But I know Carl said he was one of the easiest tests. He said it was like all the laws he'd heard about, things like that, that went on. So I guess maybe that was just a good way to prepare a setting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did Carl do? He was older than you?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Yes, about six years. Carl went to normal too and started teaching. After he taught about four years before he read his sedation in the First World War, I think he was starting his fifth year. Yes, I know he was. Because he wasn't in shape to take a physical examination for a while, he'd been playing ball over in Montana, baseball over in Montana, and stuffed a leg, a sliding vase over there. And he was hobbling around when it was time for school, and he, well after he seemed to be healed up by the end of the Navy, about November of that year. And then when he came back, he also finished college. And he taught most of his life after that. First he was up here, then he went down to a few places around for a few years, then he settled in California and he was in Santa Maria for 20 odd years until he retired from that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Joe continue teaching after he got his degree?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh yes, he continued until he was... Oh, he spent in all... Let's see, he spent, I guess, from about 1920 to 1948. He spent, I think it was, in the schools of Douglas, Arizona. He started there as a principal of the grade school and he was superintendent the last 21 years, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Paul also farmed, didn't he?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: That's what he... Yes, he farmed the home place there after he got married for quite a while. And then he bought land over in Kooskia over there, up to Clearwater.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You've been up that area, haven't you?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, this was all in Harris Ridge, as it was called. Yeah, I think he spent about 11 years over there, something like that. Then he came back over here and farmed the home place for a while and then he bought a place out near Robinson Lake. He was on that until he sold it and after that he lived in town here. He worked for the rural electrification for quite a long time. Stayed on that. He worked on that when he was farming, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about your sisters?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh, this sister became a teacher, too, but Ellen, my older sister, never did. She took a business course later on. She also was sort of an ungraded finish from school out there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she the older one?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Yeah, she was about 10 years older than Edith. So it sounds like... She married, oh, I don't know, she worked at various things like that. She kept house for people for a while and then she worked as a bookkeeper for a lumber concern out in Seattle. Eventually she died. She was about 25 when she married. She lived there. She fought out of the farm for a while. Then most of her life was in construction work, building construction. They lived in Lewiston for quite a long time and then moved down to San Jose, California. That was about 1944 they went down there. They lived there the rest of their lives. It sounds like the older kids in the family, the oldest ones, got less education. Yes, they did. Yes, all of us eventually became college graduates and the poor youngest. Edith waited the longest. She taught for quite a few years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you the youngest in the family?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Yes. Oh, I think, let's see, she started teaching. Well, let's see, I guess it was 1915 when she finished high school and 1925 when she... Well, 1926 when she graduated from the University of California. She'd gone there at that time. Well, 11 years, most of that 11, well, part of it was spent in school at about three different periods. And the rest of it in teaching.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, one thing I was wondering from what you were saying before, do you think it was more likely that girls would go on to high school than boys in those earlier days?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: I believe so. There was a tendency that way too. But, not such an awful tendency I noticed around Troy. There were a lot of girls that didn't go on to school. Oh, here is Edith. There you go. Thank you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There were a lot of girls that didn't go to high school too?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Yes, there were. Among the farm girls especially. Oh, I'm not so sure of that either. I remember my high school age. There was... it seems to me that girls that didn't happen. Hi. Two of them. One of them was here ahead of me. One was listening. We rented a room together in a place there. They had their own cooking room upstairs. Part of a house. They were from outside.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: From outside the Troy area?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, not outside the Troy area. Both of them lived up here on the Moscow Road somewhere. And both of those went on to school afterwards. College and so on. And they were intended to get there. And there was an awfully scant amount of kids. Well, there were quite a bunch of those that went to that school and finished the high school and went on further. If they went that far they seemed to go on. A lot of them. So there were a number of kids from Troy that... Yes, I...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: As much as a majority of the graduating class from high school?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, now all of my graduating class... I think... I think we all did. I think there were only seven of my graduating class down here. It was... they started out a good deal more if they dropped out along the way. It moved away some of them. I don't know why some of those might have gone on to school and moved away. And some just didn't. One or two died rather young. One died in the second year. He died one summer after he was here at school. But there, I think every one of those went to school somewhat… well, why...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the thinking of people like yourself? Why go to college?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh, I think about that time they were... understood more or less why you were going. By that time there was less every year about this... they saw people, others going away. There were several others that had gone away from Burnt Ridge to go to school, for instance, before I did. See, Joe was probably the first one I think that ever did, but he was nine years older than I was. In the meantime, I don't know, several of the Pearson children went away to school. Well, professionally, one of the girls became a nurse. And one of the boys became a dentist. The one that went on to school just took about a year, went to work at the bank. But progress right along when he got there. He was the cashier in the branch of the Bank of America in Los Angeles. In the Los Angeles area, at least, we tagged.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But to go to college, were people going there because they expected a career out of going? Is that the main reason?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: I think most of them, that was true. They all expected a career out of it, frankly. I tell you, that was mostly what people, the people I knew at the university were there for. They expected a career out of it. But I went to school because an awful lot of students at the school there were partly paying their own way by working, some during school years and some would lay out a couple of years and then come back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What does that mean?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: I think the boys were almost universally pointing for a career of some kind. Some of the girls may not have been. I never explained themselves to me. I think more of them just for going to college along with their friends. Or because their parents wanted them to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think they were looking at making money in the short term before they were married and that sort of thing?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, maybe some of them did. Actually, there were some of those there. There were ones like my sister who got a primary certificate for teaching the first, went out and taught and later returned. There were women in school who had three, four, five years of teaching behind them, coming back to finish out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did you decide you didn't want to be a farmer? Why did you decide you didn't want to follow in your father's footsteps and be a farmer?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, the thing is that to get farms requires money. You don't divide up those kinds of farms between children because there wouldn't be, you'd just create a hardship on them. Inviting places that are meant for one family to more than one. I knew very well of Walt and Paul, the better farmers as I'd ever been. I was always interested in the studies. I ran into schools that said you can go that way. Especially after Joe and Carl went to school. They kept harping at us younger ones to get an education.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the prospects to stay in the area? Was that important to most of the young people? Did they want to stay here?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, I guess there was a certain amount of that, yes, they would like to stay in the same area. A lot of them did. Maybe some of it was just because they had certain things to keep them here. I didn't. No, it didn't. A lot of them moved out. They usually depended on the size of the family. Five boys in our family, there were five boys in the Kelbert family, five boys in the Rees family, four in the Pearson family, four in Frank Swanson's family. Well, the great range would be about fifteen years of all those people, age range. They couldn't all stay on the farm, so some of them knew they'd have to do something else. They got out and left. All of those kind of moved out there. Herman Rubard remained on the Rubard place out there. He was the oldest boy and his mother was widowed by that time. And he married rather late in life. All the Pearson boys went. Willy and Walt would have undoubtedly stayed, but they lost health. Eventually died. Adolf farmed for a while. He said he didn't like it. He was too uncertain of life. He might have a good crop, he might have a bad. He wanted something more steady, he said.

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: And Joe never tried it. I don't think he would have cared for it either. He decided at that time the farm didn't have any attractions for him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did one of the boys take the farm?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: None of them did. They eventually sold it out to the father, Rudolph Smith, who lives there now. So there there was a farm that nobody wanted. One of the best farms none of the kids wanted to know. Well, Mrs. Pearson moved to Portland where one of her daughters was married. And Edla was down there training to be a nurse. She had a couple of the younger children with her. Her youngest daughter, I guess, then too. She was about to grow up, really. Well, she grew up down there and married. And then by that time Joe had gone and started working in the bank. And Adolf was probably about completing dentistry. It went on. There was the family, no, they just cleared out. And it's one of the best farms underage. I don't know if I had a selection of a farm on the ridge. I'm not so sure if it's the best I'd take that from. Except for one thing, they have had problems on water. And from the earth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, for yourself, could you have stayed and taught in this county, in this area, if you'd wanted to?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh, I don't know. I just took one of the first ones I had an opportunity to take. That was in the state of Washington. I found it available and I accepted it. I don't know, I didn't really want to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To stay here?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: To stay here. I wanted to try some other place. I moved around quite a bit in the early days and I wasn't too satisfied with teaching at all. So, I left teaching when I was about 35.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why weren't you satisfied with it?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh, various things. The pay was never too good, up to the time I left it. It's a tiring, worrisome job, I found it. Trying again. I teach people, interested in their work. I can see why, also, this is one of the common complaints of teachers. I think it's because the kids that you're teaching are interested in the work that they're studying. In my interest, all these are sort of an undercurrent of a little noise, things happening. I wanted you to see that they all get a chance to learn their sidings. I taught mathematics a good deal. The only thing that was definite, you knew what the kids were getting and what they weren't getting, but it was somewhat different when it came to actually teaching. A student had no mathematical sense at all. He was completely frustrated. It was too raised looking at that.

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: They kind of had to shove aside a little dirt and mud to get it into where it was. Every winter, I suppose, there was a certain amount of soil swept down that hillside. It sort of covered the dovers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Ice all year round, right?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: I wonder what it was like, what it looks like on the inside. Well, little crystals, he said, hanging in ice. There had been apparently a lot of water that had frozen at one time. It stayed that cold inside. Just what it caused, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I sort of wondered why an ice mine? Why doesn't it melt?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: But, you know, we're talking about leaving the area. I'm really interested in that because, you see, most of the old timers I talked to are the people that stayed, never left. Yes, of course, they're the ones that you'd find here. The ones I'd find, right. But that's only part of the story, of course. About yourself, you didn't really have any particular desire to stay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you think that was?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: I don't know. I don't know. I just, I don't know. Starting in on something in your own home, among your own home people, I think would be the hardest job of all. People that have known you all your life. I went to another town. I was accepted as a teacher. I tried teaching at Troy, and they'd say, here's Bud Carlson. I don't think any, some people have gone back to their own hometowns and liked it. Established themselves. Apparently that's what Roger Swanson, the superintendent, has done. But for most people, I think it's better to be away from their own hometown. Among the home people. It can make it among strangers a lot easier.
