They might come to a family which had never been fond of another family. And those things sometimes last through generations. They're not old time Kentucky feuds, but those things persist.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That would make it unpleasant for, in your work?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: It could. It would make you unhappy personally.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about friendships though? Can you develop your friendships that are as deep as the ones that you left behind from your childhood?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: I think so. You should stay aligned long enough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think you might have gone into farming if you could have?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh I don't know if I would or not. Maybe I could have if I put my mind to it. That is, but I didn't really have the desire to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the trying parts of it as well as the good parts when you grow up on a farm?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Sometimes a lot of work you put in for nothing is thought over again. The poor crop yields, the poor price years and so on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you agree with that Pearson boy that said that it's too risky?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Yes. He had something there. He said he didn't think he'd care to farm. He tried it for a year. He'd been in high school and he'd studied agriculture and some agriculture courses there and he got really interested in how it ought to be done. When he faced a practical problem of getting his crops harvested and his delays and getting work done due to weather and all that. That's why he began to be concerned with the practical everyday life of a farmer. He said it didn't appeal to him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about most of the younger generation from Burnt Ridge? Most of them in the area?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Yes, I'd say so. There were four, five of the six Ruber children left. Well Herman eventually left too. But he left when he was quite a long years and he and his wife sold out. He went to Pullman and he worked a little while longer around. He worked for the state college for a while until he retired. But, oh, yeah, this all went out to work. Clarence became a barber. Victor became an automobile mechanic. The two young husbands, Hugo and Richard, went to college. Hugo went to work for the gas and electric company in California and spent many, many years at it until he retired. Richard worked for that same company for a while but then he experimented with other things. He ran a taxi cab service for a few years. Then he went back for a refresher course in education and finished as a teacher. The girl, she worked out in Spokane for quite a while. She was a housekeeper for a family and then she married up there.

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: And married again after her first husband died. Yes, I was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think girls left the area as much as boys did?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, a lot of them left, yes. Some married men that lived here and a few lived on here. Well, a lot of them left. Some had something to do. The woman had property in the area or something like that. They stayed for other reasons, I guess. Some stayed with the folks a long time when they were old and they were getting older. Then there were quite a few of the families that moved out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the others?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Calbert's daughters, all except Helen, who married Ernie Anderson and brought out the other heirs that was going to settle up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was the only one that stayed here?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Well, Elvera married a man who became a missionary. And one of them married a man that lived, she lived in Moscow and still does. She went out of school too after, both before and after she married. And got to work at the university there. I think she was a lab assistant. Something I'm not sure what she was. One of the boys tried farming. The two of them tried farming at home, farming out there. One of them went out to get a ready to have ready land elsewhere and one quit to become a mechanic. And one of them married a girl and continued to live right there on the farm. She helped land. One boy went back east. He wanted to become a minister but he found a grind for the education. He left there, I don't know how he did. He worked as a truck driver part of the time. And he had a shop at one time, one of his brothers told me, to put me a pair of shavs, two of the pair of shavs.

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Scattered out. Another one tried farming too for a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Of the Kelberts?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Yes. And the two left went up to work in Spokane. That was a kind of work. It sounds like maybe getting a college education was somewhat unusual though for the younger generation on birthdays. Well yes it was. I guess for the ones below my age and below my age it increased. For my age and older it was rather rare. So it was a trend towards getting a college education. A trend. The two youngest Rubeard boys were younger than I. The rest were older. The two youngers went to college. None of the oldest of the Kelberts children went but, well, Helma started it actually. She started it late after she became nearly a young woman. Well, after drop down, well Teddy went for a while but he left it. He left the high school. He did go for a while and then dropped out and tried first farming and then got a job in Spokane which suited him better because he stayed there from World War II onwards. Not about that time.

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Late, Russell, well there was Elphaba. She's another one. She and Helma were the ones in that family that really persisted in school. But it was it. And Alder sent his children there. The older girl was older but she was one handicap in her youth by having a feeling she had to stay home after her mother died with all the younger children around. She probably started before I did. But the younger ones went on to school a good deal and Gladys, who didn't marry, made a profession out of it. Well, sticking to the families there. One of Paul Redine's boys went on to college. The other didn't because there was more questions of choice I think. And the children of those fellows who stayed here and married, they all seemed to be going on to school, a lot of them. And little Ed Swanson's girls went on to school too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: College?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: College, yes. I don't know how many were college graduates. Well, Rowena went into nursing. It's different. But I think one of them married too young to finish college or maybe even gotten there. But there were others who I guess are more. Well, if you try to put on your statistician's hat and think about it in a very rough way, how would you size up the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Of your generation, would you guess one out of four would go to college maybe?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: I doubt it. No, I don't think so. Probably maybe one out of six or... Yes, I don't know. The percentage of the populations in school or in certain age groups was pretty low about the time I started college. I suppose maybe you could find the statistics, but it was rather low. Now I'm thinking in terms of the area, in terms of Burton Beach and even the Troy area. Well, I don't know. It just differed too much from the general trend. I suppose they sent a certain number of you to school too. I know. In the classes I went to in high school, several of those people went on to school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what about leaving the area? How about that? Do you think there was a large percentage of the younger generation left and left this county?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh, I think there must be quite a few, yes. There are some that have been there a long time, but there are... If you mean back 40, 50 years, there's a lot of them and never people that have come in since that time. But if your age group of the younger, the kids of the farms on Burnt Ridge, probably a pretty solid majority of them left and didn't stay. Oh, yes. I'd say a solid majority left. If you're gonna say not... The Smiths' family stayed. Two of them are still around and one was here a large part of his life too. One moved down to Oregon. I think he was just farming down there. And the girls both married and none of them are farmed right in the fifth and since it's anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you were gonna guess, and I know this is just guessing again, but would you guess maybe four out of five would leave for the younger generation? Four out of five?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh, I don't know. Maybe three out of four. If the 20 generation dovetails into another, it would be a little hard to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think of the trend towards staying or leaving and change through time? Or do you think it's always been a high out-migration?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: I think it's been an out-migration regularly. It used to probably not so much if there's a... Well, the families are smaller nowadays than they were then. My family's birth rate is 8, 5, 12, 6, 9, 5, 6, 5, clearly everybody. Four would be about the smallest I can remember except for one or two kids. Just three in one. But most of them are high numbers, what we call high numbers now. I've got another thorny question along those lines. You're thinking about the impact of the car.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the car made it easier for people to stay in the area because it could go farther to work? Or do you think it made it more likely they'd leave the area because they could get away easier?

speaker: Gustav Carlson
words: Oh, I don't know if it had that much effect. The car, I think it was a little easier on them. They liked life a little better. They didn't spend as much. They could get places quicker. I don't know. I think it tended to make life more livable for the farmers. Get out a little once in a while easier. I think that helped to keep people.
